name: Acharuli Khachapuri
servings: makes 6each enough for a substantial snack
intro:
With its endless affinity for all things freshly baked, Jerusalem boasts the highest ratio of bakeries to residents in Israel. Naturally, it didn’t take long before locals adopted another way of enjoying the seductive combination of baked dough and cheese, to add to their fascination with burekas.

Tango Shavit, a Jerusalemite of Georgian origins, is the man behind the Khachapuriah, a small bakery that opened several years ago near Machne Yehuda market, specializing in the traditional Georgian cheese-stuffed khachapuri, the recipes for which have been passed down through his mother’s side of the family. The acharuli—a boat-shaped khachapuri baked with an egg on top—is one of the dishes most readily associated with the city’s Georgian Jewish community.

The acharuli is also another product of the “eating on the run” culture, an attitude, possibly a way of life, that has been taken to new heights in Jerusalem. In a place where manners and decorum are at the absolute bottom of the priority heap, all that is really required of you is to avert disaster. Hence the awkward, often comical angular eating postures that have been developed in order to avoid anything edible landing on one’s shoes or clothes: a cheesy egg yolk from a khachapuri, some tahini gathered at the bottom of a pita with falafel, amba (mango pickle) seasoning, the shawarma that’s been carved and folded into flatbread, or the tomato paste threatening to run loose from a quivering hot burekas.
tips: These are like bread rolls that are stuffed and baked with lots of cheese inside. Serve them as a snack or a simple meal alongside a fresh salad. They are fun to make but require a little bit of confidence with dough. The tricky part is shaping them with a large enough cavity to hold an egg. If you are not feeling up to this challenge, just leave out the eggs. But they do add a wonderfully rich creaminess, so we urge you to be daring.

ingredients.item: scant ½ cup / 110 g Greek yogurt
halloumi cheese, cut into ¼-inch / 0.5cm cubes
chopped thyme, plus extra to sprinkle

method: Start with the dough. Sift the flour into a large mixing bowl and add the yeast. Mix lightly. Create a well in the center and pour in half the egg (keep the other half to brush the rolls later), yogurt, and the lukewarm water. Sprinkle the salt around the well.

Begin stirring the mix, adding a fraction more water if needed (not much; this dough should be dry), until everything comes together in a rough dough. Transfer to a work surface and knead by hand for 10 minutes, until you have a soft, elastic dough that isn’t sticky. Return the dough to the bowl, cover with a tea towel, and let rise at room temperature until doubled in size, 1 to 1½ hours.
Knead again to knock out the air. Divide the dough into 6 equal portions and roll each into a ball. Place on a lightly floured surface, cover with a towel, and let rise for 30 minutes.
To prepare the filling, combine all the ingredients except the eggs and olive oil and stir well. Place a baking sheet in the oven and preheat to 425°F / 220°C.
On a well-floured surface, roll the dough balls into circles 6½ inches / 16 cm in diameter and about ⅙ inch / 2 mm thick. You can do this with a rolling pin or by stretching it with your hands.

Spoon roughly one-sixth of the cheese filling onto the center of each circle and spread it slightly to the left and to the right so it almost reaches the two edges of the circle. Take the right and left sides between your fingers and pinch them as you stretch the dough a little to create an elongated, boat-shaped pastry with the cheese in the center. Straighten the side walls and try to make them at least 1¼ inches / 3 cm high and wide, so that there is enough space in the center to hold the cheese as well as the whole egg that will be added later. Pinch the ends again so they don’t open up during the cooking.

Brush the rolls with the remaining half egg and put them on a sheet of parchment paper the size of your baking sheet. Sprinkle some thyme leaves over the rolls. Remove the baking sheet from the oven, quickly place the parchment and rolls on the pan, and put the pan straight back in the oven. Bake for 15 minutes, until the edges have a golden tan.

Remove the baking sheet from the oven. Break an egg into a small cup. Without breaking it, gently lift the yolk with your fingers and place it in the center of one of the rolls. Pour in as much of the egg white as will fit, then repeat with the remaining eggs and rolls. Don’t worry if some egg white spills over; it’s all part of the rustic charm. Return the pan to the oven and bake for 5 minutes. The egg whites should be set and the yolks should remain runny. Allow to cool for 5 minutes before you drizzle with olive oil, sprinkle with salt, and serve.
